subject: dentistry
title: Dental disorders
preambleMarkdown:
# Dental disorders



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Dental disorders is a clinical condition or syndrome in dentistry.
- This article focuses on oral findings, dental procedures, infection risk, bleeding risk, medications, and referral triggers.
- The practical clinical priority is dentistry diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Dental disorders to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Dental disorders is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is dentistry diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Dental disorders depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In dentistry, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Dental disorders.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Dental disorders disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Dental disorders should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Dental disorders first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Dental disorders, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Dental disorders, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused dentistry examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Dental disorders presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Dental disorders with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Dental disorders is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Dental disorders should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Dental disorders is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses dentistry diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Dental disorders, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Dental disorders may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Dental disorders patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Dental disorders, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Dental disorders in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Dental disorders, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Dental disorders does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Dental disorders may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Dental disorders, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Dental disorders as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Dental disorders draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Teeth, Maxilla, and Mandible

_Source lines: 24168-24551._

```text
V. TEETH, MAXILLA, AND MANDIBLE
A. Dental anatomy. The tooth consists of a crown and root. The crown consists of
the enamel, dentin, and pulp above the gingiva. The root has no enamel, and the
dentin is fixed to the periodontal ligament by the cementum. The neurovascular
bundle leaves the root at the apex. Identification of the teeth for charting pur-
poses is illustrated in Figure 12-9.

B. Dental caries (cavities) result from bacterial erosion through the enamel into the
dentin and pulp.

1. Clinical features
a. Symptoms. The chief complaint is usually a "toothache" or jaw pain. The
pain is exacerbated by hot and cold liquids or food. The tooth is often sen-
sitive to touch as well. Pain can be referred to the jaw or ear.

b. Physical examination reveals pitting of the tooth surface. Percussion of the
tooth or probing of the caries may elicit pain.

2. Differential diagnoses include apical abscess, periodontal abscess, and fracture
of the tooth. Myocardial infarction can present as tooth or jaw pain and should
be considered in a patient who is short of breath or ill-appearing and in whom
no caries can be readily identified.

3. Evaluation. Physical examination is the sole means of evaluation.

4. Therapy. In the ED, supportive care can be provided (e.g., analgesics, local an-
esthesia with a lidocaine or mepivacaine injection at the root of the tooth, and
placement of dental wax in the cavity to reduce sensitivity).

5. Disposition. Patients are discharged with instructions to see a dentist as soon
as possible.

C. Dental abscesses
1. Discussion. A tooth that has caries is susceptible to infection.

a. Apical abscesses occur when the pulp is infected and the infection pro-
gresses along the root to form an abscess at the apex of the tooth.

b. Periodontal abscesses result from infection of the gum or periodontium.

300 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Eye, Ear, Nose, Throat, and Dental Emergencies
2. Clinical features
a. Symptoms. The chief complaint is usually a "toothache" or pain in the
gums.

b. Physical examination findings. A fluctuant, tender gingival area at the base
of the infected tooth is palpable.

3. Differential diagnoses include caries, gingivitis, and gingival foreign bodies.

4. Evaluation is by physical examination.

## 5. Therapy

a. Drainage of the abscess is best performed by incision with a no. 15 scalpel
blade.

b. Antibiotic therapy. An antibiotic effective against anaerobes, such as
penicillin VK (250 mg four times daily) or clindamycin (300 mg four
times daily), should be administered.

6. Disposition. Patients can be discharged with instructions to follow-up with a
dentist.

D. Trauma
1. Dental trauma
a. Tooth fractures. Dental trauma can lead to a tooth being chipped or bro-
ken. The Ellis classification scheme is used to classify dental fractures
(Figure 12-10 and Clinical Pearl 12-3).

i. Evaluation entails careful inspection. The tooth should be blotted
(to improve visibility), but never probed, because probing can intro-
duce bacteria to exposed pulp.

ii. Therapy
a) Ellis class I fractures are a minor problem and often do not re-
quire any treatment. If there is a bothersome sharp edge, it can be
rounded with an emery board.

b) Ellis class II fractures in older children and adults should be cov-
ered with calcium hydroxide and a dressing. In children, some
class II fractures require emergent treatment by a dentist to reduce
the risk of infection.

c) Ellis class III fractures should be treated emergently by a den-
tist to reduce the risk of infection. Often, a root canal must be
performed.

F I G U R E

Eye, Ear, Nose, Throat, and Dental Emergencies
iii. Disposition. Generally, patients with Ellis class I or II fractures are
referred to a dentist for follow-up the next day. Patients with class III
fractures, children with class II fractures, and patients with suspected
root fractures should be referred emergently to the dentist.

b. Tooth subluxation or intrusion
i. Discussion
a) Subluxation occurs when an injured tooth is loose or displaced in
the socket.

b) Intrusion occurs when a tooth is impacted in the socket.

ii. Clinical features. The tooth may be loose, painful, and
maloccluded. On physical examination, the tooth will be loose
and it may be impacted into the gum. Often, there is blood in the
gingival crevice.

iii. Differential diagnoses. Subluxation must be differentiated from root
fracture and avulsion.

1. Ellis class I fractures. Patients complain of a chip or a sharp edge, but no pain. Physical examination
will reveal the chip.

2. Ellis class II fractures. Patients often complain of sensitivity to changes in temperature or to air.

On physical examination, a yellow spot (i.e., the dentin) in the center of the fracture is visible.

3. Ellis class III fractures can be painful because the nerve is exposed. On physical examination, the
fracture has a pink center (representing bleeding from the disturbed blood vessels and nerves in the
pulp). Class III fractures that occur at the root are often missed because the tooth may seem to be intact.

Therefore, any tooth that is loose or painful after trauma should be evaluated by a dentist.

PEARL	CLINICAL 	12-3
F I G U R E
12-10 Ellis classification scheme for dental
fractures. In Ellis class I fractures, the
enamel is fractured. In Ellis class II
fractures, the enamel and dentin are
fractured. In Ellis class III fractures, the
enamel, dentin, and pulp are fractured.

(Courtesy of Brenton Kathman.)

Ellis class I
Ellis class II
Ellis class III
Alveolar fracture
302 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Eye, Ear, Nose, Throat, and Dental Emergencies
iv. Evaluation often requires dental radiographs, which are not available in
most EDs.

v. Therapy
a) Reduction is often painful, and anesthesia (provided by a lidocaine
injection at the root) may facilitate the process. Once reduced, the
tooth can be immobilized with dental wax.

b) Analgesia should be provided.

vi. Disposition. Patients should be referred to a dentist for definitive treat-
ment as soon as possible.

c. Tooth avulsion occurs when a tooth is knocked out of its socket.

i. Differential diagnoses. Avulsion must be differentiated from an alveolar
fracture.

ii. Therapy. An avulsed tooth is a true emergency and must be reimplanted
as soon as possible. Each minute that the tooth remains out of its socket
reduces the likelihood of the tooth surviving by approximately 1%. It is
important to determine whether the tooth is a primary (baby) tooth or
a secondary tooth. Primary teeth are not reimplanted because they often
ankylose or fuse to the bone, causing permanent deformity.

2. Maxillary fractures
a. Discussion
i. Etiology. Most maxillary fractures are the result of motor vehicle collisions.

ii. Types include:
a) Simple alveolar fractures, which run through the alveolar portion of
the maxilla, where the teeth are implanted
b) Antrum fractures, which are fractures of the maxilla at the base of
the nose
c) Le Fort fractures (Figure 12-11)
b. Clinical features. Le Fort fractures are usually a combination of the three
types (e.g., one type may be seen on one side of the face and another type
may be seen on the other; see Clinical Pearl 12-4).

c. Differential diagnoses include orbital blow-out fractures, orbital tripod
fractures, fracture of the antrum of the maxillary sinus, and Le Fort type I
and type II fractures.

d. Evaluation. A CT scan is the standard of care and yields the most informa-
tion because the three-dimensional image allows clear visualization of the
fracture.

e. Therapy
i. Airway control is the most important priority and should be accom-
plished early, before airway obstruction is present. It is much easier to
intubate a patient when the airway is moderately swollen; when the
airway is obstructed, cricothyrotomy often becomes necessary.

ii. Control of epistaxis is often difficult. If packing is unsuccessful, emer-
gent reduction of the fracture may be needed to control the bleeding.

iii. Fracture reduction and internal fixation are accomplished by a facial
specialist.

iv. Antibiotic therapy is needed to minimize the risk of central nervous
system infection.

f. Disposition
i. Alveolar fractures should be stabilized using dental wax or arch wires.

Urgent follow-up with an oral surgeon is necessary.

ii. Antrum fractures can be repaired on an outpatient basis if no other
injuries are present.

iii. Le Fort fractures. Patients require admission for open reduction and
internal fixation. Often, these patients have other injuries and require a
complete trauma evaluation anyway.

3. Mandibular fractures
a. Discussion. Mandibular fractures (Figure 12-12) are relatively common and
are usually the result of a direct blow.

E Y E , E A R , N O S E , T H R O A T , A N D D E N T A L E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	303

Eye, Ear, Nose, Throat, and Dental Emergencies
F I G U R E
12-11 Le Fort fractures of the midface. In
Le Fort fracture type I, the maxilla is
separated from the upper face by a
horizontal fracture though each nostril
above the hard palate. In Le Fort fracture
type II, the fracture is oblique and runs
from the nasomaxillary area through the
orbit, separating the zygoma and orbits
from the nose. In Le Fort fracture type III
(craniofacial dysjunction), the facial
bones are separated from the cranium.

The fracture runs through the maxillary
sinus, orbits, and zygoma.

II
I
III
F I G U R E
12-12 The most common locations of mandibular fractures
are the body, the angle, the condyle, and the symphysis.

In more than 50% of patients, the mandible is broken in
two places.

304 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Eye, Ear, Nose, Throat, and Dental Emergencies
Types of Le Fort Fractures
1. Le Forts type I fractures
a. Symptoms include malocclusion (manifested as an inability to bite) and facial tingling (as a result of
infraorbital nerve involvement).

b. Physical examination findings include a bilateral epistaxis, a long-appearing face, a mobile hard pal-
ate, and crepitance. Airway obstruction may occur secondary to swelling in the area of the soft palate.

2. Le Fort type II fractures
a. Symptoms include malocclusion, facial tingling, and often diplopia.

b. Physical examination findings
i. Bilateral epistaxis, periorbital ecchymosis, and a long-appearing face are noted.

ii. The central region of the face will be mobile between the bridge of the nose and teeth, and crepi-
tance is present.

iii. A step-off defect at the inferior orbital rim, a medial canthal deformity, enophthalmos, and restricted
extraocular motion (resulting from entrapment of the inferior oblique muscle) may be noted.

iv. Airway obstruction can occur as a result of swelling in the nasopharynx.

3. Le Fort type III fractures
a. Symptoms include malocclusion, tingling in the face, diplopia, and facial pain.

b. Physical examination findings
i. Bilateral epistaxis and cerebrospinal fluid rhinorrhea are noted.

ii. The face is unstable, moving laterally through the orbits with stress.

iii. Marked facial swelling, ecchymosis, a medial canthal deformity, a lateral orbital rim defect, and
enophthalmos may be noted.

iv. Significant retropharyngeal swelling can lead to airway obstruction.

PEARL	CLINICAL 	12-4
b. Clinical features
i. Symptoms include pain, the inability to open the mouth, malocclusion,
and tingling of the lip if the inferior alveolar nerve is affected.

ii. Physical examination findings
a) Trismus, deviation of the jaw, an inability to bite down, and
malalignment of the teeth are usually noted. Lip and chin sensation
may be impaired.

b) In patients with a condylar fracture, blood may be noted in the
external auditory canal.

c) The teeth should be inspected. Bleeding at the base of a tooth
signifies an open fracture through the socket.

c. Differential diagnoses include an alveolar fracture, dislocation of the jaw,
and trismus from a zygoma fracture.

d. Evaluation. CT scan is again the standard method and provides more ana-
tomic information in terms of displacement and angulation.

e. Therapy
i. Fractures that displace the mandible forward are more amenable to
treatment because the muscles help to stabilize the fracture. These
fractures can be stabilized with arch wire supports to the teeth. The
patient should be placed on a diet of soft foods.

ii. Fractures that displace the mandible backward are more difficult to treat
because the masseter, medial pterygoid, and temporalis muscles displace the
fracture. These fractures often require open reduction and internal fixation.

iii. Open fractures. Prophylactic antibiotics must be prescribed to prevent
infection of the bone.

f. Disposition
i. Discharge. Patients with closed fractures amenable to treatment can be dis-
charged with instructions to follow-up with an ear, nose, and throat specialist.

ii. Admission. Patients with open fractures that are amenable to treatment
require admission for prophylactic antibiotics and open irrigation of the
wound. Patients with complicated fractures also require admission for
open reduction and internal fixation.
```
